questions.question: What decomposition do both voice cloning and voice conversion depend on?
options: Factoring a waveform into (content, speaker, prosody) and recombining — cloning takes text + a reference voice; conversion takes person A's speech + person B's voice and outputs B saying A's words
questions.explanation: Same factorization, different input side: cloning is TTS-side (text in), conversion is speech-side (audio in). In 2026 a 5-second clip suffices for a high-quality clone on consumer hardware.

questions.question: What legal constraints does a 2026 cloning pipeline ship under?
options: The EU AI Act (enforceable August 2026) and California AB 2905 (effective 2025) require inaudible watermarking and consent gates — the pipeline must refuse non-consensual clones
A global ban on all voice synthesis
Only a copyright notice in the metadata
questions.explanation: Watermarking (PerTh, SilentCipher embed ~16-32 imperceptible bits surviving re-encoding) and tamper-evident consent records are pipeline requirements, not bolt-ons.

questions.question: What separates zero-shot cloning from few-shot fine-tuning?
options: Zero-shot needs no reference audio at all

Zero-shot conditions a multi-speaker-trained TTS on a speaker embedding from a 5-second clip (F5-TTS, XTTS v2, OpenVoice v2); few-shot LoRA-fine-tunes on 5-30 minutes of the target for about an hour, jumping from "okay" to "indistinguishable"
They differ only in output sample rate
questions.explanation: SECS above ~0.70 is generally indistinguishable from the target for most listeners — zero-shot F5-TTS already hits 0.72; fine-tuning buys consistency.

questions.question: What are the two voice-conversion families?
options: Recognition-synthesis (extract content via ASR-like representations such as PPGs, resynthesize with the target speaker embedding — robust across language/accent, e.g. KNN-VC) and disentanglement (autoencoder bottleneck separating content/speaker/prosody — faster, lower quality, e.g. AutoVC)
questions.explanation: Recognition-synthesis routes through a content representation that has already discarded speaker identity; disentanglement asks the bottleneck to do the separation.

questions.question: How well do synthetic-voice detectors work against modern cloners?
options: ASVspoof 2025 published EERs of 0.8-2.3% for state-of-the-art detectors (AASIST, RawNet2, Wav2Vec2-AASIST) against ElevenLabs, VALL-E 2, and Bark outputs
questions.explanation: Detection, watermarking, and consent records form the defensive triad — the same neural-codec advances that made cloning easy (VALL-E 2, VoiceBox at SECS 0.77-0.78) made detection benchmarks essential.
